subject: Re: Offer Letter
body_text:
I wish you all the luck in the world. But I don't think you'll need it,
based on all the competence I have seen so far. :-)

Scott

On Thu, Jun 12, 2025, 6:59 AM Ashli Shiftan <ashli.shiftan@scale.com> wrote:

> Hi there -
>
>    1. There is a lot that goes into the 409a work which I would leave to
>    our Chief Accounting Officer but I can tell you at a high level, the strike
>    price is simply a portion of the share price on the market which is in that
>    $17.44 that Mike put in the model. Our last round it was $5.21 and the
>    share price was $14.55 so around 35% of the share price. The $7.00 is 40%
>    but we will continue to negotiate that down which is why we believe that
>    will be the high strike price. Dennis is planning to speak to you when
>    things calm down a bit so perhaps first thing tomorrow and he can walk you
>    through this and the deal terms.
>    2. @Michael Weiss <michael.weiss@scale.com> Mind doing that as the
>    dust settles this morning? Thank you!
>    3. @Michael Weiss <michael.weiss@scale.com> can also weigh in here but
>    we had that in there previously I believe with an estimate of being able to
>    sell at around 2 years. Likely around when we would be looking at an event
>    again anyway.
>
> Thanks Scott! Wish us luck today!
>
> Ashli
>
> On Thu, Jun 12, 2025 at 6:11 AM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
> wrote:
>
>> Thank you both very much. Really, this is so helpful, despite everything
>> you have going on.
>>
>> A few q's
>>
>> 1. What would need to be true for the strike price to land at $7. Why
>> would that be true?
>>
>> 2. The way I am thinking about things (and would love your feedback) is
>> to think about the share price in terms of what you'd have to believe the
>> company is worth (at those share prices). Perhaps you could add in a new
>> row with valuation into your scenario planner in the top right corner? This
>> would let me play with different positive and negative scenarios.
>>
>> 3. There is a new column "eligible to sell" I presume this is related to
>> the liquidity preference/deal terms. But perhaps I have that wrong/
>>
>> Thanks,
>>
>> Scott
>>
>> On Thu, Jun 12, 2025 at 12:12 AM Michael Weiss <michael.weiss@scale.com>
>> wrote:
>>
>>> Hi Scott,
>>>
>>> We have updated your calculator based on 1.5% of the new share pool
>>> post-dilution (~25m options). You'll see a few choices on the right side
>>> you can plug in to model out price scenarios:
>>>
>>> Share price:
>>> - $17.44 is the stock price paid by Meta in this deal
>>> - $14.55 is our most recent preferred price prior to the deal
>>>
>>> Strike price:
>>> - $7.00 is our high end estimate of where the strike could land after
>>> the deal
>>> - $5.21 is our most recent strike price prior to the deal
>>>
>>> To value your equity I've defaulted to the most conservative combination
>>> ($7.00 strike and $14.55 preferred). You can edit prices in yellow cells to
>>> model different outcomes.
>>>
>>> Please let me know if you have any questions. Happy to chat through this
>>> live tomorrow if helpful.
>>>
>>> Thanks,
>>> Mike
>>>
>>> On Wed, Jun 11, 2025 at 11:25 PM Ashli Shiftan <ashli.shiftan@scale.com>
>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hi Scott - Following up on our conversation, we have the calculator
>>>> <https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/14kI-ISr5y3bm9iXLXHUUotiZydW2ROEOGZGAQYnGfws/edit?gid=1973658010#gid=1973658010>
>>>> ready but I am adding in @Michael Weiss <michael.weiss@scale.com> here
>>>> who leads compensation for the company because he can add more color here.
>>>> He is much more articulate than I am on these things :)
>>>>
>>>> I am working on other things we discussed as well and will get them to
>>>> you tomorrow.
>>>>
>>>> Ashli
>>>>
>>>> On Wed, Jun 11, 2025 at 4:30 PM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
>>>> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> I had a careful read through the offer and got some legal advice.
>>>>>
>>>>> Can you help me see how things work out with the options under the new
>>>>> valuation and the new circumstances?
>>>>>
>>>>> Unlike the previous offer, the options have no value when I join and
>>>>> may not have a lot of value for quite some time while we rebuild the
>>>>> company (which I'm excited to do). For me, I'm risking about $100M over the
>>>>> next 4 years. Thus, I wonder whether you can paint me a path where this
>>>>> works out positively or be covered for the risk. Perhaps you can paint some
>>>>> scenarios?
>>>>>
>>>>> The 4 areas I'm worried about are
>>>>>
>>>>> - Things don't work out
>>>>> - Acquisition (and termination)
>>>>> - (Some) Liquidity along the way.
>>>>> - Meta acquires > 50% and decides not to sell "forever"
>>>>>
>>>>> We can talk tonight - I'm talking to Jason at 5:15.
>>>>>
>>>>> Thanks,
>>>>>
>>>>> Scott
>>>>>
>>>>> On Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 9:15 AM Ashli Shiftan <ashli.shiftan@scale.com>
>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>
>>>>>> I'm doing well, would be great if you accept :) Jason is very close,
>>>>>> will keep you posted. I think the team could be incredible with you and him
>>>>>> at the helm.
>>>>>>
>>>>>> On Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 8:39 AM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Hi
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Lawyer believes turn around will be tomorrow. I will push for sooner.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Hope you are doing ok.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Scott
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> On Mon, Jun 9, 2025, 8:59 PM Ashli Shiftan <ashli.shiftan@scale.com>
>>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Hi there! This sounds great, thank you for the update. Just shot
>>>>>>>> over the financials from Dennis and he is standing by to give you an
>>>>>>>> update.
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Jason has not yet made his decision but we are seemingly getting
>>>>>>>> closer. Happy to chat through live as well here. Will be available for you
>>>>>>>> all day tomorrow.
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Ashli
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> On Mon, Jun 9, 2025 at 8:52 PM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
>>>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Hi Ashli -
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> I'm talking to a lawyer early tomorrow AM and expect to have
>>>>>>>>> concrete feedback then.
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> I expect most of my conversation with him will be about (1) the
>>>>>>>>> risks of things not working out for me and the company given all the
>>>>>>>>> changes and what I'd be giving up at Google to take that on and (2)
>>>>>>>>> liquidity concerns in the face of valuation volatility during this
>>>>>>>>> valuation volatility/reset (NB: you folks have already, I believe,
>>>>>>>>> protected me well from valuation volatility)
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> I plan to be in touch in the first half of the day.
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Need to chat before then, please feel free to call or text. Did
>>>>>>>>> Jason make his decision yet?
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Thanks,
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Scott
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> On Mon, Jun 9, 2025 at 12:34 PM Ashli Shiftan <
>>>>>>>>> ashli.shiftan@scale.com> wrote:
>>>>>>>>>

> You mean, you folks will meet without me tomorrow?
>
> I have 830 to 930 PT free at the moment.
>
> Scott
>
> On Thu, Mar 5, 2026 at 8:53 AM Salvador Ramirez <
> salvador@landmarkswest.com> wrote:
>
>> Good morning, our plan is to meet tomorrow and then provide you the info
>> requested.
>>
>> We should be ready for Monday for a meeting. Is there a specific time?
>>
>> Thanks,
>> Salvador Ramirez
>> Project Manager
>> 435-503-4620
>> https://landmarkswest.com
>>
>>
>>
>>
>> On Wed, Mar 4, 2026 at 10:49 PM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
>> wrote:
>>
>>> Thanks for your email.
>>> Hi this is a good start - 13K. What more can we do?
>>>
>>> I did some staring at this try to guess at what was driving the
>>> increases in the New construction section:
>>>
>>> I see 241 convenience outlets? Do we need that many is this code or
>>> preference (if so, who's preference?)
>>> I see USB-C things. We need zero of those.
>>> Lutron keypads - assuming these are crazy expensive, any way to value
>>> engineer those?
>>>
>>> I think on the panels, you have already done this work? Trying to figure
>>> out what options there are here, if any. In general, I would have been fine
>>> with older panels.
>>>
>>> I'm more than happy to get on a call, but I'd prefer to understand what
>>> the main cost drivers are and what real options we have. Would it be
>>> possible for you to share some more of that and we can schedule something
>>> for MOnday?
>>>
>>> Thanks,
>>>
>>> Scott
>>>
>>> On Wed, Mar 4, 2026 at 7:40 AM Nicolas Johnston <
>>> trailtechelectric@gmail.com> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hi Scott, hope this finds you well.
>>>>
>>>> I wanted to explain the panel relocation and panel replacements.
>>>>
>>>> The main house has one mechanical room that houses three "main" 200Amp
>>>> house electrical panels. There is an additional 200Amp main panel in the
>>>> library garage (now workshop) that also feeds a 100Amp sub panel.
>>>>
>>>>   In order to save cost, we relocated one 200Amp main panel from the
>>>> mechanical room to the storage room under the breezeway (closer to the
>>>> addition). The reason being, is that the length of wire was not sufficient
>>>> and would have likely been more costly due to the power needed for the
>>>> addition.  For example, we needed 2 80Amp steamer circuits for the
>>>> addition.  With the additional circuits needed for power , as well as the
>>>> lighting system requirements (adding a lutron lighting panel for the
>>>> addition), it was more cost efficient to relocate a panel to this end of
>>>> the house, saving on wire as well as the labor it would take to fish
>>>> circuits across the basement.
>>>>
>>>>  In order to relocate a panel to the mechanical room to the breezeway,
>>>> we needed to combine two panels inside of the mechanical room. The existing
>>>> panels were 40 circuit panels while the updated panels we installed are now
>>>> 80 circuit panels. After inspecting the remaining panels (1 in large
>>>> mech.), we agreed that in order to ensure longevity of the home, it was in
>>>> the best interest to replace all electrical panels. The breakers were
>>>> outdated as well as a lot of the large breakers being loose and indicating
>>>> wear and tear. Throughout this entire project I've tried to prioritize
>>>> budget when it comes to decision making and overall cost efficiency while
>>>> maintaining our Trail Tech standard.
>>>>
>>>> Also, I want to give a few suggestions to help with budget/scope.
>>>>
>>>> Beam Accent Lighting Replacement:
>>>> Elevator Beams: (8)- Beams- 7' each (56' total) (2) LED Drivers (8) LED
>>>> Starters
>>>> Great Room Beams: (8)- Beams- 4' each (32' total) (2)- LED Drivers (8)-
>>>> LED Starters
>>>> Sky Bridge: (8)- Beams- 7' each (56' total) (2)- LED Drivers (8)- LED
>>>> Starters
>>>> Joy's Office: (6)- Beams- 4' each (24' total) (2)- Beams- 7' each (14'
>>>> total) (8)- Beams- 10' each (80' total) (5)- LED Drivers (16)- LED Starters
>>>> 11,176.00
>>>> I would suggest not replacing all beam accent lighting, saving $11,176.
>>>> I think we have plenty of efficient lighting, decorative lighting, and
>>>> accent lighting without replacing the beam accent lighting. This is a labor
>>>> heavy project.
>>>>
>>>> Additional Recessed lights above beam locations: Add additional
>>>> recessed lights above beam locations for additional lighting.
>>>> Elevator Stairway: Add (20)- 4" recessed lights
>>>> Library: Add (9)- 4" recessed lights Move (3)- 4" recessed lights
>>>> Skybridge: Add (4)- 4" recessed lights
>>>> Also, I would suggest not adding additional recessed lights to these
>>>> areas. This would be VERY labor intensive, and I think these are areas that
>>>> can be overlooked.
>>>>
>>>> Theatre Tread lighting: Add tape light to Theatre stair treads.
>>>> 36'- 24V LED tape light
>>>> 1- LED Driver
>>>> 8 starters
>>>> $2,180
>>>>
>>>> This is another area that we may be able to cut costs?
>>>>
>>>> Please let me know if you have any additional questions. I would be
>>>> happy to get on a video call with you sometime and give further explanation
>>>> or to brainstorm ideas to help with the scope.
>>>>
>>>> Thank you and I hope you have a great day!
>>>>
>>>>
>>>>
>>>> Nic Johnston
>>>> Trail Tech Electric
>>>>
>>>>
>>>>
>>>> On Sun, Mar 1, 2026 at 3:51 PM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
>>>> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> Hi there -
>>>>>
>>>>> The vast majority of out of budget items appears in the "New
>>>>> Construction" area,
>>>>>
>>>>> 1) without any size of these line items, so it's hard to tell where to
>>>>> start cutting scope (maybe starting with "low", "medium", "high" could be
>>>>> helpful)
>>>>> 2) without clarity about which of these are allocated to "inital
>>>>> scope" vs "expanded scope" (e.g. added rooms and areas) it's hard to tell
>>>>> how much of this is driven my "more area" vs "mis estimates" vs "expanded
>>>>> scope in initial scope areas"
>>>>> 3) Also can you tell me about the Panel replacements and relocations.
>>>>> Which are those and why do they need to happen?
>>>>> 4) Finally, if you have some ideas on scope reduction / value
>>>>> engineering, please suggest them.
>>>>>
>>>>> Thanks,
>>>>>
>>>>> Scott
>>>>>
>>>>> On Tue, Feb 24, 2026 at 12:17 PM Salvador Ramirez <
>>>>> salvador@landmarkswest.com> wrote:
>>>>>
>>>>>> Hi Scott, hope you had a great vacation. Keep us updated on this when
>>>>>> you get a chance.
>>>>>>

body_text:
Time to get a Mustang like Sherri

On Tue, Dec 28, 2021 at 8:07 AM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com> wrote:

> Second half of 2023!!
> And I bought the package they optimized for !!
>
> Scott
>
> ---------- Forwarded message ---------
> From: Rivian <hello@e.rivian.com>
> Date: Tue, Dec 28, 2021 at 8:01 AM
> Subject: Your Rivian R1T estimated delivery window
> To: <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
>
>
> A note to our Adventure Package preorder holders
> [image: Rivian R1T]
> <https://click.e.rivian.com/?qs=750bdf6fc2c5bcf5762ee08d66a2a774cf99ec6ed0498ce08089a0c075222b303c173dfe893bf9737a2905b504f179d79460de668388071f42e94eb7a6f9fa39>
> Hi Scott,
>
> Thank you for your preorder and your commitment to helping us create a
> more sustainable world. Now that production is ramping up at our plant,
> we’re able to provide a more accurate estimate of your delivery window.
>
> *Your Adventure Package R1T estimated delivery window will be the second
> half of 2023.*
>
> We realize this estimate may be different than what you had originally
> expected. Your delivery window estimate is based on your preorder date,
> delivery location and your current configuration. Due to the high volume of
> preorders for our Adventure Package with Large pack battery, we will be
> focusing on that configuration in 2022. In setting our delivery timing, we
> optimized our build sequence around the build combination that would
> support us ramping as quickly as possible and therefore have the largest
> possible positive climate impact.
>
> To bring added transparency and better understanding to delivery timing,
> in early 2022 we are introducing a new feature on your Rivian Account Page
> that will display your current delivery timing estimate. Our goal is to
> give you accurate, up-to-date details about your specific preorder so you
> can better plan and stay more informed.
>
> Your preorder remains a vote for finding better ways of doing things on a
> planet that needs our collective care and attention. If you have any
> questions, please reach out to us
> <https://click.e.rivian.com/?qs=04490d96d6ead90e45829c42128a695c62b8416a25c24f1962fc4166823b7b26eae3f2341b05ecf1282207190c17ca2de05858e95f48b764e619837ae5a1e0bb>

subject: Re: Quizlet signon help
body_text:
(thanks Nestor for helping, greatly appreciated).

On Tue, Sep 23, 2025 at 5:22 PM Nestor Hernandez <nestorh@gmail.com> wrote:

> Thanks everyone for your patience here.
>
> We believe we've addressed the issue for this user, resetting SSO and
> password which should give them a clean slate. Bao we suspect this could be
> the result of a user id or email migration within the Google Workspace
> domain.
>
> Support should have reached out to Bao and Jason, please let us know if
> things are not working from your end.
>
> Wishing everyone a good schoolyear!
> Nestor
>
>
> On Fri, Sep 12, 2025 at 12:44 PM Nestor Hernandez <nestorh@gmail.com>
> wrote:
>
>> Hey Bao,
>>
>> Just checking in. We have a few candidate solutions to address this, but
>> could use a bit of additional info to narrow it down as we work in
>> parallel. A HAR file running through the flow, or even screen capture of
>> what is happening. Some of the interactions are with systems outside of our
>> control including Google Signon and your workplace config.
>>
>> Thank you,
>> Nestor
>>
>> On Mon, Sep 8, 2025 at 8:52 PM Nestor Hernandez <nestorh@gmail.com>
>> wrote:
>>
>>> To help us debug, could you record a HAR file while running through the
>>> signin sequence? This helps us understand what's going on even if the
>>> traffic is getting blocked or hitting other systems than our own.
>>>
>>> Instructions for recording a HAR file can be found here.
>>> https://developers.cloudflare.com/support/troubleshooting/general-troubleshooting/gathering-information-for-troubleshooting-sites/
>>>
>>> Thanks!
>>>
>>> On Mon, Sep 8, 2025, 8:44 AM Nestor Hernandez <nestorh@gmail.com> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Thanks Bao, looks like the setup is in a much better state now with
>>>> Quizlet account emails getting through. Seems like the account got into a
>>>> strange state. I'll take a look with the team. If there are any other
>>>> accounts in this state please let me know
>>>>
>>>> On Fri, Sep 5, 2025, 2:33 PM Bao Huynh <bhuynh@lasdschools.org> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> Thank you Jason!
>>>>>
>>>>> Hi Nester/Scott,
>>>>>
>>>>> There seem to be something with Quizlet and lasdk8.org account
>>>>>
>>>>> When we tried to login to quizlet using "google" with
>>>>> 27ap25547@lasdk8.org it said the account already exists and we have
>>>>> to put in a password. There is no password on the account so we did the
>>>>> "reset password". The magic link let us in the account but when we tried to
>>>>> create a password for it, it said the "google account is wrong". Is there a
>>>>> way to fix this or the student will have to do the "reset password" every
>>>>> time?
>>>>>
>>>>> Thanks,
>>>>>
>>>>> On Fri, Sep 5, 2025 at 2:28 PM Jason Post <jrpost@gmail.com> wrote:
>>>>>
>>>>>> Hello Bao,
>>>>>> Adding +Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com> (Alex's Uncle) and +Nestor
>>>>>> Hernandez <nestorh@gmail.com> (from Quizlet) who has kindly agreed
>>>>>> to look at this integration.
>>>>>> Thank you.
>>>>>> Best, Jason
>>>>>>
>>>>>> On Fri, Sep 5, 2025 at 1:40 PM Bao Huynh <bhuynh@lasdschools.org>
>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Hi Jason,
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Students' lasdk8.org accounts do not get email from outside the
>>>>>>> domain and thus it did not get the "reset password" magic link. I have
>>>>>>> allowed account@account.quizlet.com so your son should be able to
>>>>>>> get the magic link in his lasdk8.org email and get to Quizlet.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> However, I now see that there is some "mismatch" between his
>>>>>>> lasdk8.org account and quizlet. Can you get me in touch with his
>>>>>>> uncle to the Quizlet eng team to fix this?
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Thanks,
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> On Fri, Sep 5, 2025 at 12:29 PM Jason Post <jrpost@gmail.com> wrote:
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Hello!
>>>>>>>> Thank you.
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Screenshots HERE <https://photos.app.goo.gl/1puJoxWdCmRTQB8eA>
>>>>>>>> Here is the flow -
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> 1. login using SSO with an existing account
>>>>>>>> 2. password is wrong (lost / forgot)
>>>>>>>> 3. Request password reset link
>>>>>>>> 4. Unable to receive password reset link in inbox
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Best, Jason
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> On Thu, Sep 4, 2025 at 11:47 AM Bao Huynh <bhuynh@lasdschools.org>
>>>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Hi,
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Quizlet is not blocked for students. Can I have a screenshot of
>>>>>>>>> the issue?
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Thanks,
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> On Tue, Sep 2, 2025 at 3:45 PM Jason Post <jrpost@gmail.com>
>>>>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Greetings Mr. Huynh,
>>>>>>>>>> I hope you are doing great.
>>>>>>>>>> My name is Jason.
>>>>>>>>>> My son Alexander (27ap25547@) is a student at Egan.
>>>>>>>>>> For his school work, Alex is asked to use Quizlet
>>>>>>>>>> <27ap25547@lasdk8.org>.
>>>>>>>>>> When he tries to login to Quizlet using his LASD login, he is not
>>>>>>>>>> able to gain access.
>>>>>>>>>> Using his personal  credentials, he is able to gain access.
>>>>>>>>>> On the user side, we have not been to able to get Quizlet working
>>>>>>>>>> for LASD.
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Being asked to use Quizlet while not being able to access it as a
>>>>>>>>>> signed-in student has been a friction point for Alex and his classmates.
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Alex's Uncle has a relationship with a member of the Quizlet eng
>>>>>>>>>> team who is glad to help work on the LASD <> Quizlet integration, *would
>>>>>>>>>> you be open to being connected to him? *
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Thanks for considering.
>>>>>>>>>> Best, Jason
>>>>>>>>>>

subject: RE: Dartmouth business follow-up and send off invitation
body_text:
Great, I’ll send in her RSVP would it just be Gigi?
Jinny


From: Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com>
Sent: Tuesday, July 30, 2024 8:54 PM
To: j. quinones <j.quinones@dartmouth.edu>
Cc: Joy Silver <joysilver@gmail.com>
Subject: Re: Dartmouth business follow-up and send off invitation

Yes gigi would like to go.

On Tue, Jul 30, 2024 at 8:19 PM Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com<mailto:scottmsilver@gmail.com>> wrote:
Thanks. I'll ask Gigi. She has her first day of internship that day, so I'm not sure we'll be able to make it.

Thank you for asking!

Scott

On Mon, Jul 29, 2024 at 12:46 PM j. quinones <j.quinones@dartmouth.edu<mailto:j.quinones@dartmouth.edu>> wrote:
Greetings Scott & Joy,

I just returned from a trip to Hanover last week. I connected with my colleague, who is still working on securing dates from Professor Balkcom, stay tuned. I also did not see your RSVP for the sendoff reception this week on Thursday, August 1. I’m resending the invite just in case it fell off your radar.  Please let me know if I can be helpful and RSVP on your behalf.
With gratitude,
Jinny

From: j. quinones
Sent: Tuesday, June 25, 2024 11:16 AM
To: Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com<mailto:scottmsilver@gmail.com>>
Cc: Joy Silver <joysilver@gmail.com<mailto:joysilver@gmail.com>>
Subject: RE: Congratulations and Dartmouth business follow-up

Hi Scott & Joy,
Let me contact my colleague and ask if time this week would be possible. If not, I will request a few dates in July and August. Once I have a few options, I will contact Becky. I will also take care of the gift processing on our end.
Thank you,
Jinny



From: Scott Silver <scottmsilver@gmail.com<mailto:scottmsilver@gmail.com>>
Sent: Tuesday, June 25, 2024 9:49 AM
To: j. quinones <j.quinones@dartmouth.edu<mailto:j.quinones@dartmouth.edu>>

subject: On Curve Steepeners, Subway Surfers, and Such
body_text:
https://paulkedrosky.com/r/4fe1bbbd?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f











On Curve Steepeners, Subway Surfers, and Such [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/512b03d2?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f]









Thinking like a macro hedge fund manager about the US eating itself









By Paul Kedrosky • 16 Jul 2025


View in browser [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/1e362038?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f]




View in browser [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/12225ac4?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f]


















 











 















It is the usual drill: First, some updates on prior posts, then the latest essay, and, finally, some links that I want to unburden myself of—and that some of you may find interesting.


Updates and Erasures:

I wrote [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/d3528001?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] not long ago about higher education and its attempts—with unintended consequences—to find new ways to finance operations, given higher endowment taxes and disappearing grants.

Well, the number of schools newly issuing debt continues to grow [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/f72d442c?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f], with Yale now joining in. This latest issuance takes higher education muni bond sales to more than $24-billion so far this year, sharply higher than in 2024, and the highest since 2014.

As ever, much of the fun is in the supporting quotes. Here, from the linked Bloomberg piece, is an S&P ratings person:

> “Dartmouth maintains sufficient liquidity and is assessing financial mitigations to provide it with flexibility to navigate these changes”

That is a clever obfuscation for "sweet mother of god, they have a hell of a liquidity problem". Again, see my prior piece [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/0d15b1fa?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] for how and why that is likely to get worse before it gets better, which it likely won't.

> “Issuing bonds remains a common, prudent way for institutions to fund significant capital projects as part of responsible financial planning"

This, from a Dartmouth spokesthingie, is also terrific work. It harkens to the "I'm not funny looking, you're funny looking" sort of middle school rejoinder, which will never not be childishly appealing to me. He is saying that anyone who thinks it's weird to issue bonds is themselves weird, which is fair—but higher education has been much more sparing in the recent past, which is ... noteworthy and will have consequences.


Rough Notes:


How to Think About the Monetary Policy Apocalypse

A month or two ago, I was having a Thai lunch with my friend Jeff, a former hedge fund manager who now mostly plays professional poker and has Thai lunches. We were speculating about under-priced risks, and there are many to choose among, given our current polycrisis of polycrises.

I gave him my then-favorite candidate: The current White House would find a way to fire the Federal Reserve chairman, Jerome Powell, despite a recent Supreme Court ruling saying he couldn't. Doing so, I argued, would cause a hot (unpriced but tradable) mess.

It admittedly hasn't happened yet¹ (even if it's apparently imminent [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/85d27bdd?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f]-ish). There has been a parade of officials dropping highly wordsmithed hints that they think Powell has broken the law vis-a-vis his upholstering choices, so he should be fired for renovation cause. Why for renovations, not rates? Because the Supreme Court has said a Fed chair can only be fired "for cause", and that has to do with fraud, managerial incompetence, etc., not rates higher than some real estate developer would like. So, office renovations it is.





¹ The word "yet" is doing a lot of work in the above sentence. Current administration policy can seem like a fat-fingered octogenarian accidentally launching apps while scrolling an iPhone. WTAF IS THIS? SUBWAY SURFERS? I DIDN'T TAP THIS! Well, yeah, you did, kinda.






There must, one might think, be something one can do about it. How, in short, to make money from such silliness? Kudos for your sociopathy: This is thinking like a macro hedge fund manager, and thinking like this can be fun, or at least a decent distraction from doomscrolling into the abyss.

For example, say you knew copper tariffs were coming in the U.S., which, if you were paying attention, you knew as far back as February of this year. As soon as you heard it, you began contracting with shippers and rerouting copper [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/310a71c5?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] supply from useful things to your in-U.S. stockpiles, so you can sell it later at post-tariff high prices. That is why now, months later, there is more copper stockpiled at U.S. ports than at Shanghai, Rotterdam, and Singapore ports combined.

So, kudos for doing that, which you didn't, but would have if you had access to container ships. It has only been called the "metals trade of a lifetime".

This is what it is like cosplaying macro hedge fund manager. And it is a useful distraction from ... all of this [Paul waves his arms at modernity]. It is, in its way, analogous to my current bet on Jonas Vingegaard in the Tour de France: I don't want him to win, but by betting on him to win, I have a stake in an outcome that would otherwise be horrible to me, which makes me feel better while he not-loses.

But let's return from copper to monetary policy. Cosplaying a macro hedge fund manager here, the key insight from our Thai food (fine, it's well understood and has been for decades) is that if U.S. monetary policy becomes overtly political, it will undermine expectations of Fed independence, which in turn distorts the term structure of interest rates. This is a fancy way of saying that investors won't trust the Fed to not do stupid and political things.

For example, markets may begin to price in what's euphemistically called persistent policy error: being wrong, knowing you're wrong, and staying wrong. The government might leave rates low despite high inflation, because that is fun; they might raise rates late in election cycles to screw over the next Administration, because that too is fun.

Of course, many will say, So, same as it ever was. After all, the Fed was too low for too long after Covid, which helped make inflation worse, and the current administration has convinced itself that rates are too high because ... because, well, they're too high.

The impact of such persistent errors is non-linear. If the Fed is seen as a political plaything, long-dated government debt (i.e., the long end of the Treasury curve) will likely rise relative to the front end. Why? Because the front end reflects immediate policy, which may still follow data (or may not, as seems currently likely). But the long end captures inflation expectations and the risk that shit goes bad while you're holding (the "term premium"). Both are highly sensitive to perceived credibility.

Nevertheless, if you think rates are going to be flat or declining at the short end, and high and rising at the long end, you've just talked yourself into a so-called steepener trade. A steepener is a position that benefits if the yield curve steepens—i.e., if the spread between long-term and short-term interest rates increases. There are various kinds, but we only care about what's called a bear steepener, where short rates flatline or even fall, while long rates rise.

There are lots of ways to do this, from long/short ETF positions in treasury-related ETFs, to futures, to going Full Burry, getting an ISDA [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/a6de9f5f?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f], and convincing a broker to create something expensive for you. I'll leave that as an exercise for you and your favorite LLM.

Because it probably won't work anyway. First, timing is everything. There is a reason steepeners are called "widow-makers"; you bleed a little every day in hopes of one day getting a huge infusion of EPO [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/2da5e339?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f]. Maybe you will, but mostly you won't.

Second, and like with hoarding tariff-ed copper, firing a Fed chair is no longer news. The current administration has nattered about it publicly for months. The market knows it is likely coming, so, on the news, you could see the curve flatten rather than steepen, which would be eye-rollingly bizarre, not to mention wreck your trade. But that's how markets work. You need to do this sort of thing when everyone thinks it's a stupid idea, not when it's obvious. Of course, that could be only 10-20 milliseconds from now in the current milieu. See also: Subway Surfers above

Meanwhile, of course, U.S. monetary policy is going to become about as reliable as Cher from Clueless: Fickle, whimsical, whipsawed by moods and fashion. And that will have few consequences until it does. While a steepening trade is seductive, because it feels intelligent, sophisticated, and timely, it is also likely near-term wrong—even if it's also likely long-term correct about the eventual consequences of politicizing monetary policy.


Even Rougher Notes:

The demographic collapse [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/796cd64c?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] and its consequences … Ageing rates [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/bea58f97?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] vary globally—politics implicated … Neural networks [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/4f621b90?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] get topographic upgrade to mimic human vision … Global economy [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/9273b83a?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] somehow keeps not failing despite crises and uncertainty … Mobile money [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/6587f5bd?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] boosts African savings to decade high … Cars keep exploding into flames in UAE heat wave [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/efc9db12?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] … Red meat [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/995650bd?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] linked to rheumatoid arthritis risk … Why do China stocks fall at night? [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/fbc41b80?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] … Junk bonds [https://paulkedrosky.com/r/bd767dbb?m=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f] hit record trading volumes.



















Paul Kedrosky © 2025 – Unsubscribe [https://paulkedrosky.com/unsubscribe/?uuid=22dd5e75-bc28-4662-bfd8-9900cdad8c6f&key=14a89b081d2acab9f682985c1895a193f28f82a20b59b908ca76c1d0c4f7ebf1&newsletter=6012a013-ce25-4a8b-afad-f67ab3796b21]

subject: New in Opik: Auto-Optimize Multi-Step Agents, Annotate & Score Entire Chatbot Convos, & More
body_text:
Dear Scott Silver, When building agentic systems, it’s often the full conversation, not just individual LLM calls, that reveals how well your applications are working. That's where the latest round of Opik releases comes in, making it easier to evaluate and optimize entire sessions to improve your AI applications' performance at a higher level. Working with AI chatbots? Now you can capture entire multi-turn conversations, invite human experts to review and score them, and run conversation-level eval metrics like user frustration and conversational coherence. Opik’s agent optimizer SDK is ready for more complexity too, with the ability to go beyond singular prompts and perform automated optimization runs on multi-step agents. Read on for more details and tips on using these new features – plus, check out how Zencoder relies on Opik to build and test their fully agentic software pipelines, and discover where to connect with fellow AI developers in the upcoming weeks! Learn more Opik SDK Thread Evaluation Now, when you run multi-turn conversations through Opik, the platform will automatically group related traces into conversation threads. To evaluate and optimize your conversation threads using the new [evaluate_threads] function in the SDK, specify a filter to apply metrics like user frustration and conversational coherence to specific threads. You’ll receive an evaluation report generated locally within the SDK, containing all evaluated threads within your agent system, with immediate visibility of the report within the Opik UI. View docs Thread-Level Feedback Scores Opik’s new thread-level expert feedback feature is now available! This feature has been tailored for subject-matter experts to review entire chatbot conversations in context, flag insights and risks, and collaborate directly with dev teams. In addition to manual scoring, you can now tag threads and leave contextual comments to enhance collaboration and provide greater clarity within workflows. View docs Agent Optimizer 1.0 You can now automatically optimize not just single prompts, but full agentic systems! With built-in support for LangGraph, Google ADK, PydanticAI, and more, this release simplifies the API, allows you to bring your own model to evaluation, and separates the optimizing LLM from the evaluation LLM for more control. View docs Insights From the Comet Team Dmitrii Krasnov, Engineering Manager at Zencoder, shares how his team utilizes Opik to build and scale Zencoder—an AI-powered code assistant capable of everything from real-time code repair to autonomous JIRA ticket resolution. Learn how Opik has improved research efficiency and provided full trace visibility and faster iteration across daily experiments for the Zencoder team: Read here Connect & Learn with Fellow GenAI & ML Developers Join us live in the coming weeks for the following conferences, workshops, prizes, and opportunities to connect with fellow AI builders: - ICML 2025 (International Conference on Machine Learning) – Vancouver, July 14th-17th - AI Tinkerers NYC Hackathon – NYC, July 19th-20th - Las Vegas Comet X Weaviate Hacknight – Las Vegas, August 12th
Comet builds tools that help data scientists, engineers, and team leaders manage any ML or LLM lifecycle, from training through production. We provide an end-to-end model evaluation platform, with best in class LLM evaluations, experiment tracking, and production monitoring. Organizations of every size—from academic teams and startups to enterprise companies—use our platform. Start building with us today!

Comet ML Inc., 228 Park Ave S, Suite 15549, New York, NY 10003, United States

subject: A Free Press Live Debate on Foreign Policy
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://www.thefp.com/p/a-free-press-live-debate-on-foreign

The specter of World War III looms larger than ever.
Just a week ago, Iran launched its largest-ever ballistic missile attack against Israel in what has become a multifront battle [ https://substack.com/redirect/3f114f3d-7b79-423c-ba43-e6a3a2b4b4ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] in the region. The grinding war of attrition in Ukraine rages on, with over a million [ https://substack.com/redirect/069ca2e0-9f81-4000-9a35-b603ac8f3791?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] people killed or injured since Russia’s invasion in 2022. China has been attacking [ https://substack.com/redirect/e9407a35-12ff-4e14-b654-baa3ceffef32?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] Philippine and Vietnamese vessels in international waters with impunity. 
The question is: What should America do about it? 
On Wednesday evening, more than 800 people packed into Symphony Space in Manhattan for a Free Press debate about American foreign policy. Bret Stephens and James Kirchick faced off against Matt Taibbi and Lee Fang to hash out the question: Should the U.S. still police the world? 
At the beginning of the debate, a whopping 81 percent of the live audience sided with Stephens and Kirchick, voting in favor of a hawkish foreign policy. Throughout the night, there were tense exchanges over past conflicts in Iraq, Afghanistan, and Vietnam, the virtues of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, and whether it is even ethical for journalists to advocate for sending young, working class Americans into conflicts abroad. And though the discourse was heated, it was also civil.
“We force our belief systems upon others at the barrel of a gun. When we act unilaterally and attempt to use the awesome might of the American military and intelligence forces for regime change, we tend to have disastrous results,” Fang said in his opening statement. 
“With due respect to European multilateralism, the idea that some condominium of French diplomats working in tandem with our good friends in Moscow and our partners in China is going to solve our security crises is a fantasy. It’s a nice one, but it’s false,” Stephens said later in the night.
By the end of the debate, Taibbi and Fang were able to convince 2 percent of the audience to switch to their side–that the U.S. should not continue to police the world—changing more minds than their opponents—and thus winning the night. 
The Free Press is honored to have partnered with the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression [ https://substack.com/redirect/486c0294-7be2-48fc-9c30-81ab26135ee9?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] to present this final installment of The [ https://substack.com/redirect/70261fb3-fca8-4fba-a4c0-c46eb1cdc97c?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] America [ https://substack.com/redirect/647a052b-bfd1-41b2-a836-2a825422e575?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] Debates [ https://substack.com/redirect/7a98c788-6847-4f9e-884a-67746c7594d1?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ], a series on the issues that matter most to voters this upcoming election cycle. 
For those of you who weren’t able to join us, paid subscribers of The Free Press can watch the full video of the event above. So if you’re not already a paid subscriber, become one today.

Unsubscribe https://substack..kPatTDV03HmnoWDT_DsHR30SdGWJ1I5yEwNPQtHRdqA?

subject: RE: Completion of 2021 accounting

Collette has been working on the reconciliations and she found that the QuickBooks upload link for the Delta Sky Miles credit card (#1018) is not working.  It looks like it stopped uploading toward the end of October.  When you have a minute, would you mind looking into it to see if you can get it reconnected?

Thanks for your help and happy weekend!

Stephanie

Subject: RE: Completion of 2021 accounting

Hello Scott,

I hope all is well with you and your family.  Thank you for checking in with us.  Your timing is impeccable!  Our January proved to be a bit challenging and we are finally catching up.  Collette was just letting me know that her desk is thinning out and she is ready to go.

I have accessed the November and December statements and have updated the spreadsheet.  The only one that we need you to reload is the January 2022 loan statement for the 8976 loan.  The copy in the 8976 folder is the statement for the other loan.  Also, if you could upload the January statements would be great.  We will need to capture any December activity reported on the January statements.

Collette will be working on your accounts today and tomorrow, so let’s schedule a call for next week and talk through your items below.  That way we will have QuickBooks up-to-date and can see how 2021 is shaping up.

Also attached is the engagement letter for 2022.  If all looks good, please sign and return to me for our files.

Thanks and talk to you soon,

Stephanie

I hope that you had a wonderful holiday season.  Just checking in with you regarding the completion of the account reconciliations for 2021.  I think that we are in need of the November and December bank and investment account statements and the credit card statements for period close dates in December and January (as the statements close for capturing December activity.)  For the Platinum card, we need the November statement as it closed later in the month.

I have looked over the balance sheet and P&L for 2021 and overall they look pretty good.  There are a few P&L accounts that stand out a bit from the prior year that we will want to have you look at.  For instance, automobile expense in high for 2021, but it looks like there may have been a Tesla purchase or down payment. Please let me know if you want to work some more on the balance sheet to keep getting it closer to representing your assets.

subject: An Interview with Vercel Founder Guillermo Rauch (Stratechery Interview 6-8-2023)
body_text:
Good morning, This Daily Update interview is another installment of the Stratechery Founder series; as a reminder, one of the challenges in covering startups is the lack of available data. My solution is to go in the opposite direction and interview founders directly, letting them give their subjective overview of their companies, while pressing them

Good morning,

This Daily Update interview is another installment of the Stratechery Founder series; as a reminder, one of the challenges in covering startups is the lack of available data. My solution is to go in the opposite direction and interview founders directly, letting them give their subjective overview of their companies, while pressing them on their business model, background, and long-term potential.

Today’s interview is with Guillermo Rauch ( https://twitter.com/rauchg ) , founder and CEO of Vercel, along with the open source Next.js framework for React. Next.js allows front-end developers to built React-based web applications with server side rendering that are dynamically tailored to individual customers; those applications can be easily deployed using Vercel, without any need to pre-configure or provision cloud instances. Vercel’s last raise of $150 million was in November 2021, at a valuation of over $2.5 billion.

In this interview Rauch traces not only his history, but also that of React and Next.js, and explains why Vercel and Next.js have always been inextricably tied together. We discuss whether or not it is a business challenge building on open source, and why Vercel can re-sell cloud services with a healthy margin without its customers fleeing. We also touch on Vercel’s go-to-market, fundraising in 2021, and Rauch’s ambitious vision for the future.

To listen to this interview as a podcast, click the link at the top of this email to add Stratechery to your podcast player.
