*ES:* It could be. Look, it makes total sense for Etsy to partner with OpenAI ( https://www.etsy.com/news/meeting-buyers-where-they-are-etsy-partners-with-openai-to-enable-ai-powered-shopping ) , I think it probably makes sense for Shopify ( https://www.shopify.com/news/shopify-open-ai-commerce ) , but again, there’s idiosyncratic qualities for all these partnerships that I think you have to look at.

Etsy, you wrote about this, they’re all long tail, I bought a beautiful handmade watch case from Etsy, it took three months for the guy to make, and it’s got my initials engraved on it. That’s not a high volume econ product so it makes total sense for them to embrace this partnership just for the discovery aspect.

Walmart, there’s some questions around whether they are actually doing the account linking as a result of joining the partnership. If they’re doing that, then they do kind of have more of the first party relationship, but at least they’re getting the customer data. So what OpenAI is saying in this documentation is that, okay, you’re going to get some customer information as a result of servicing the transaction because you are the merchant of record, but that doesn’t mean you get to use it, you can’t use the email for marketing.

Well, the email for marketing is actually really important piece of data. When you go to a Shopify website, what’s the first thing you see within five seconds? It’s pop-up asking for your email, giving you a discount. So we know how to price that, but we know how to price that, it’s worth that much, it’s worth 15%, 20% of the AOV [Average Order Value] right then and there because that email is everything, that email is the anchor to your identity, that’s how they find you on Facebook, that’s how they re-market to you, that’s how they promote to you. And if you can’t use it in that way, then all you’re really getting is that one incremental conversion.

*This part’s really, really important by the way. I actually wrote about this years ago ( https://stratechery.com/2016/google-and-the-limits-of-strategy/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..X5_aExoazViZiHINByZW6lJIbxGNgV5CkGk7nf8oF-KQhhFAbEHIuKdRg-m14YaXCViwpUqQ8GlxAZ2suPf4tC8pmDw5LbGkQZvrqBKHBjPEyp_LC2eZJPI2-EIIX3Vh3aTHNVCCOUTGvSKubPR8Qvgcgyfu4pdgAg6uBBPz92juSr5V60ozYFOw9RyWp-hZELJK4mZ9jMayweOiAs7YzHWiAITi73IJU_HVN7r0sYBKJE2SHFct6cd8MGTznwjD__ZDMM1WNM297DX0TYKy-rph0Grdzdbo3EfcRgLwBpCozBqedpcP2o0PfaVhPdFav1fSAEApGl4tdNosa7IiTg ) , and I am actually just remembering that — man, I should have brought this up again previously, I could have beat you to the punch! This bit about the value of a bid is predicated on is it a one-time conversion or is it an opening to a long-term relationship? And that opening to a long-term relationship is insanely valuable, and this is always the problem.*

*Actually, this was when I was more bearish on Google back in the day, this was the core of my thesis. This was back when Google Assistant or whatever, seven, eight years ago, I think I was writing about this, the problem with thinking, “Oh they can just do affiliates”, is if a Google customer feels like all they’re doing is converting a sale and they don’t have any prospect of a future relationship, the value they’re going to place on that bid is going to be significantly lower and that’s a huge problem. Yeah, I’m just saying what you just said, but it’s a super important point.*

*ES:* The bids are not priced based on one-time AOV bid, they’re priced on the cohort value, the LTV [Lifetime Value]. The LTV is a huge topic of research and marketing, it’s the lifetime value of the user, so projected, and they use PLTV, so projected lifetime value that’s projected out on whatever timeline you want, but oftentimes it’s like for e-commerce and DTC, it’s 30 days. For other types of products, for B2B it’d be many years, but for a lot of digital apps it’s 90 days, 360 days it could be, I mean it could be years — I’ve seen cases where they have PLTV that’s projected out years, and that’s what the bid is.

*One of my all-time favorite DTC products, I’ve been buying shirts from them now for six years, whatever they bid to get a hold of you the first time, they got a deal, I’ll tell you that much.*

*Well, so on this point, this is sort of a theoretical question. Do you think we’re going to have fewer products going forward or more? If we do get in a world of LLMs helping direct users — this is not the first version of this question, this came up with the Internet the first time around where, “Now everyone’s just going to find the best product and you don’t need to do as much marketing”, that obviously proved to be totally false, the cost of acquisition actually went up because the breadth of possible items sort of went up. But do you think it’s going to be a similar story with LLMs?*

*ES:* Well, it depends. So actually I make this point in the article that will be the result if they stick with the affiliate model and they don’t embrace ads, because what ads do is they allow new products, new competitor products, to be discovered using that bid mechanism. So you can overcome the lack of conversion data with the bid mechanism because the system’s going to prefer — I mean, there’s ways to counter this with explore exploit bandits ( https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.01867 ) type systems to give other products a chance, but the reality is they’re going to converge around the products that have the best conversion value.

*And that’s a hard thing to do at scale, you’re asking yourself to systemically under-monetize so that you can bring in new bidders to the market in the long run. Which is a calculation that is very nice in theory upfront when you’re setting it up and once you’re reporting to investors, kind of goes out the window real quickly.*

*ES:* Exactly, there’s institutional reasons why that might be problematic, and so I think the reality is if you stick with the affiliate model, you’re going to have far fewer because you’re just going to converge around the products that convert the best. If you have ads, the size of the digital advertising economy or growth in digital advertising economy should say is correlated with expanding product choice for consumers, this is established in the academic literature. This has opened up a universe — just think about the existence of the DTC category is totally predicated on Facebook ads, and that’s not a controversial statement, I think that’s widely accepted.

*This is actually one of my questions, is I think you can measure the health and power of an advertising platform based on the extent to which they create their own advertisers and Meta is obviously the king of this and this is why they were so resilient in the face of — I remember when the COVID stuff first went down, and Facebook earnings came out, and I’m like ( https://stratechery.com/2020/facebook-earnings-auction-dynamics-full-steam-ahead/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..IEYqBS66mAIIGMl_kTS2Mmgxho4GUpNqSe154ZdVP-Chkuhfo9yWRtTfVFPSv7OG5WeDu4x0pBtx0X_PVWA8tuen-ylKP67uSh8in1jzNCHYACY2gqo72_DYCSpEJxskHk8RdKbq9dWihFR449_GFy3O_HV_fWllfPsKLE1JrBXnSoTS7XALdf429jOQ-qBgdYw--yYmU641w0FvconURFA0ozntQ4HYtoryyLjyO7M2YJIYnb36ZV9pMq8Vf2Kf3uRntqIVyEVQnrfMV0wiwmWcKXV7U_wivI3F2zW2B3MtWcyOMW0LGCiD7ggbyoHoAl9i8k2xb3y8M-Uy0Iuj_A ) , “Look, this company is totally fine because all their advertisers, if they don’t advertise, will die, so, they’re going to keep advertising”. This is before obviously it took off, and then everything went sort of crazy but is there a world where you see sort of ChatGPT doing that, where there’s entire companies, entire categories that never would’ve existed without LLMs and LLM advertising?*

*ES:* I think it could be, I think if they find a really clever — and look, they’ve got brilliant people working on this as we speak, they’re going to launch this, it’s not a question of if, it’s a question of when and I think it’s probably going to be pretty soon. But the thing is, if they can marry that product use case, the high engagement focused product use case within an ads product that just works very productively with it, they will have a massively expansive ads platform. And the thing is, what I don’t think people fully appreciate — and this is not to switch topics to Meta, but just to invoke Meta briefly.

*Oh no, they’re next because there’s the obvious tie in so feel free, you can handle the transition.*

The State of Meta

*ES:* So, I did this solo podcast recently called Commerce at the Limit ( https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/season-5-episode-27-commerce-at-the-limit/id1423753783?i=1000715326519 ) , and I talked about these AI systems, they’re obviously increasing the value of the ads, because they’re achieving better ad ranking, they’re achieving better personalization, they’re just doing a better job of matching the right products and the right person.

*And you see this, that the ad inventory is increasing even as price-per-ad is also increasing ( https://stratechery.com/2025/google-earnings-meta-earnings-the-cost-of-reality-labs/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..jUVIxo9JwoN_ZaaQFvY0ewRiHpIy3octUp-5iPQKvJr_9V1qagGUHrUUwBBcKR_OarzCujxKTjDqmUDc7Snm2Eu7kyiVzhAaToNjw1pwFsCj8cbJEzDGvo331dAp4O-6d20z_qk0UIRNHy07KJ8Wx8Ztf0Obpf0Q0HEGE2mGDuvGFqeS0fbY5C5ib6Uojzeh6r6QO0lSMt-yuyyDSBZUtxBH1txPAR6YDMXOL2Coa3xtXET6snwdVzcKE2jnDnFGnTzR3GOtsfEyPvUIWiD2eATcT6x-ap4ovCLQOH5ap1e_Uq50JcZMwPnTUPZ12edGWV4eld5UFXxAHFyF_Go9Dw ) , they’re usually going the opposite direction.*

*ES:* Right, to the highest magnitude it has, to the highest in Q3, the highest magnitude it has.

But I think what people underappreciate or they undervalue is the extent to which this is just going to onboard new advertisers that aren’t participating right now and here’s just a good example. So if you think about on the creative side, because the creative is the most visible, so I think it’s a really good place to start when you’re talking about this. People see it, people see the output.

Now, for better or for worse, there was the mini scandal that happened with the T-shirt brand ( https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-ai-generating-bizarre-ads-advantage-plus-2025-10 ) , I’m blanking on the name, but Meta created an image of an old lady, wearing a cardigan sweater, and they make T-shirts for men and they were upset about it because, well, you just hijacked our brand imagery, and you misrepresented our brand principles. But we also don’t know how well that performed, maybe it just outperforming everything, but that’s kind of an aside.

*Right, maybe the earned — or no, what’s it, the free media earned media? Whatever it is.*

*ES:* Earned media, yeah.

*The controversy may have drove more sales than anything else.*

*ES:* That could be true too, but that’s what people see, and so I think it’s an easy place to start.

And so, the thing is, think about when they first rolled this out, they had just background variations, so you have an image and you can create 10 variants with just different backgrounds so you’re getting some variation, you’re getting some diversity of creatives there, but thing is you needed an input there in the first place, and so you’re going from some small volume of creatives to a larger volume of creatives, you’re not adding a new capability, you’re just adding more variation, you’re adding more volume. But now they’ve got animations and video, now that actually is creating something for a lot of advertisers that they couldn’t afford to do so you’re unlocking a new capability.

*You’re massively increasing your pool of advertisers by doing that, particularly things like Reels.*

*ES:* Exactly. To a generally a higher performing format, which is just more higher engagement. Just think about that from the other direction there, with all the optimization stuff and also the creative capability. Now let’s say that you’re a company, and you think, “Hey, I might be able to make some money doing Meta ads or whatever ads”, and you create an account, and they’re like, “Upload your creative”, you’re like, “I don’t know what that means”. Well, set a bid, “I have no idea what that means”, define your targeting, “I have no idea what that means”, of you bring the barrier to entry so low that all you really have to do is, to the point that [Meta CEO Mark] Zuck[erberg] on your podcast ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-meta-ceo-mark-zuckerberg-about-ai-and-the-evolution-of-social-media/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..rFt1zzADK5wD8BtUee_4WD2hXY-W6vUvUbDE-j5sDWW1Oo3sNhxKSg9kaOMbpiFhnP-XBLBsNgDyaAwWPlt2faVXDRH9sjKnK4Eq4y56GS3LL9DJ36Fprf-lOUNaSVGflgr2i23gv-q5W_0HioDz09HUMngFPA7wWUoHOruVIDVER4XVfnwektUUKfUQ3gTzF5VU-Cdhgf_pIg5EqJmkEAWRWfhzHGIwHX7_upSqfErEVXpRLjP8iyx4-KT8wXJ4biLIOV97_cnuYp1rRzOu0msb2120LX5_CCCQOhXxAV37pCXEWxp6JHFujrsABS6XuSnbVSOGakgGmmC2cwYqeA ).

*Give us your website — “Here’s stratechery.com, I make $150 a year from a user”, and literally Meta can do everything else.*

*ES:* Right. And so you’ve created new advertisers, you’ve expanded the pool of advertisers, you’ve created more participation in the advertising economy, and I think that is not appreciated, and if you look at the most recent, could be the last, who knows, data that was released by the US government about e-commerce, like 16% of all commerce is e-commerce, all the rest of that is potential upside with these systems. I think that’s underappreciated, not necessarily by getting existing advertisers to convert better, or to drive more value, or drive more AOV, or transaction value, or GMV [Gross Merchandise Value], but by getting new advertisers into the system and a lot of these people are servicing a local economy, they’re not selling nationally, they’re selling to their neighborhood, and you get them into the advertising economy, and they’re probably getting better outcomes, and you’re growing that chunk of the total retail sector that it is serviced by e-commerce, by the Internet, which just in and of itself is more efficient.

*Isn’t the easiest way to summarize the eternal Meta bull case, which I think is, now we’re rounding into territory to break it out again — is it just that their advertising is simply better than anyone understands, that has been the case, will continue to be the case, it’s the case right now, and that’s sort of the point that you’re making?*

*ES:* Could be, yes, that is the bull case, there’s a bear case now, and I don’t think there’s been a bear case for a while. There is now.

*Tell me why?*

*ES:* Which is, they’re falling behind on AI.

*And why does that matter?*

*ES:* It matters because of everything I just said. If they’re not the stewards of that growth, then there’s a lot of risk for them.

*Do you see that falling behind on AI, is it about this side of the targeting, of the ad generation, all those sorts of things, or is this just the most basic problem that a company like Meta could face, which is dwindling user attention?*

*ES:* No. To be clear, in this moment right now, they’re not losing on AI, but I think they’re setting themselves up to be overtaken in the midterm, the medium-term. And why do I say that? I think a lot of the decisions they’ve made in terms of personnel are questionable.

Now, look, who am I? But I don’t know that the strategy is on its face sound and what I would worry about with that is, you’re seeing all of the productivity with the AI application now being manifested in model improvements with GEM [Generative Ads Model] ( https://engineering.fb.com/2025/11/10/ml-applications/metas-generative-ads-model-gem-the-central-brain-accelerating-ads-recommendation-ai-innovation/ ) , with Andromeda ( https://engineering.fb.com/2024/12/02/production-engineering/meta-andromeda-advantage-automation-next-gen-personalized-ads-retrieval-engine/ ) , with Lattice ( https://ai.meta.com/blog/ai-ads-performance-efficiency-meta-lattice/ ) , with better ranking — they talked about Lattice, they applied Lattice to app ads, they got 3% gain in conversions.

*Which is astronomical, by the way.*

*ES:* It’s astronomical at that scale. They’re focusing on ranking innovations with Instagram, they talked about these things. But if you lose momentum with the foundational work, well, that’s all of those gains are downstream of that, a year or two, and if you look at the stuff that they’ve been publishing on, that’s fantastic, they just published a really substantive white paper on GEM last week ( https://engineering.fb.com/2025/11/10/ml-applications/metas-generative-ads-model-gem-the-central-brain-accelerating-ads-recommendation-ai-innovation/ ) , I want to say — it was either the last week or the week before and I think the reason they did that is they have to regain the narrative here that they’re the leaders.

And I think the issue is, and you made this point ( https://stratechery.com/2025/google-earnings-meta-earnings-the-cost-of-reality-labs/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..jUVIxo9JwoN_ZaaQFvY0ewRiHpIy3octUp-5iPQKvJr_9V1qagGUHrUUwBBcKR_OarzCujxKTjDqmUDc7Snm2Eu7kyiVzhAaToNjw1pwFsCj8cbJEzDGvo331dAp4O-6d20z_qk0UIRNHy07KJ8Wx8Ztf0Obpf0Q0HEGE2mGDuvGFqeS0fbY5C5ib6Uojzeh6r6QO0lSMt-yuyyDSBZUtxBH1txPAR6YDMXOL2Coa3xtXET6snwdVzcKE2jnDnFGnTzR3GOtsfEyPvUIWiD2eATcT6x-ap4ovCLQOH5ap1e_Uq50JcZMwPnTUPZ12edGWV4eld5UFXxAHFyF_Go9Dw ) , and I think you’re the only one who’s made it so far, is because Zuck doesn’t want to talk about ads, he wants to talk about superintelligence. But when he talks about superintelligence, people harken back to the Metaverse, this was kind of vaporware. And I think they get worried that there is no real vision here and I think if you could anchor this to, “Look at all these ads improvements”, yeah, 3%, but 3% on this massive revenue engine, it matters, and it compounds over time, and also these model improvements are happening every quarter, that is massive growth, but he just doesn’t want to talk about that and he doesn’t have a [Former Meta COO] Sheryl [Sandberg] anymore who’s willing to and I think that’s the issue. You don’t have the cheerleader for the ads engine anymore and he wants to talk about superintelligence, he wants to talk about the lofty stuff, the noble stuff, and that’s great, but I think the case is a lot harder to make on that stuff, especially with a lot of the restructuring that’s happened.

*Yeah and especially when investors really in the end did give them a break for all the spending on Reality Labs, which, when you consider the time value of money, it’s underappreciated just how much profit they’re going to have to make from this stuff to ever justify the — well into the nine figures that they’ve spent at this point.*

*But is there a bit here where the problem with talking about ads is, yes, this has always been a Zuckerberg problem, it’s been a problem internally, is him not paying that much, or not wanting to really deal with ads at all, I think the other figure here that’s figured large in this is [Meta CTO] Andrew Bosworth ( https://www.threads.com/@boztank ) , who’s controversial but has a lot of traction because he went in there and built the ad business, and got it done, and banged heads together, but is this also a talent issue — like who is really good at AI that wants to work on the world’s best ranking system?*

*ES:* Yeah, but it’s not just the world’s best ranking system. Again, a lot of these other things are downstream of that and so you get to work on foundational models, and the way you commercialize them is ads, but they get used in a lot of other ways.

So, I gave a keynote at Meta’s Performance Marketing Summit in May ( https://x.com/eric_seufert/status/1930416858743878131?s=20 ) , and we had, there’s this event before that where they had the Head of FAIR [Fundamental AI Research] talking. He didn’t say a single thing about ads, he was talking about cancer research and so the thing is, these models get used in other ways, but they start with the commercial use case of ads, which makes sense, and I would make the argument that this is great for society, this is great for the economy.

*Unfortunately, you and I are just preaching in each other’s faces when we’re going on and on about how wonderful ads are.*

*ES:* But you could get someone as polished and as pedigreed as Sheryl to do that, I think that’s probably what they need to do. But the thing is, I don’t think it’s hard to point to the results, and they’ve done a bad job of that, 26% growth in the ads business, that’s the most gross since they’ve had since Q1 2024.

*Well, no, this is the whole thing — this actually goes back to the whole issue with Apple. And I think yeah, I’m going to double down on this point. A big problem why they got crushed in the PR fight with Apple, despite the fact that Apple was not just a bad actor from an antitrust perspective or platform power perspective, but literally from a good for society, they took out that ad ( https://www.inc.com/jason-aten/facebook-took-out-a-full-page-ad-slamming-apples-move-to-protect-user-privacy-it-didnt-go-well.html ) on the Wall Street Journal or whatever, about all these small businesses that Apple is putting out, and everyone sort of laughed at it, and it was 100% true.*

*ES:* True.

*Apple crushed, crushed tons and tons and tons of businesses with ATT, and got zero grief for it, and I think, yeah, there’s a bit here where, why is it fall to you and I to be sitting here and saying, “Look, we understand the ads consternation, Meta ads are genuinely good, and the benefits are widespread”, and yeah, it’s a problem that Meta doesn’t make that case, and it goes to the very top.*

*ES:* More ads, better ads, more prosperity, more prosperity, more human happiness — this is a fairly straightforward thing to grok. But look, there’s just so much of a concerted narrative pointing in the other direction, but it could be overcome, right?

*But it’s not going to be overcome if your CEO just never-*

*ES:* Doesn’t want to do it, he’s got to do the heavy lifting. Which I get it, you don’t want — and look, maybe he’s not the best messenger either, given the history.

*Yeah, fair point.*

*ES:* But you can get someone else in there though.

*It’s a good point. Are you worried at all though, just to go back to the bearest of all bear cases, about potential decreasing uses of Meta? Maybe just because people find Sora compelling ( https://stratechery.com/2025/sora-ai-bicycles-and-meta-disruption/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..J_7Lwz8wAslAG6tUjlp3Uok9SZYwFsO-qDLxFIGr0ZUsmuYdi728GRSXtPPLJTJWelNbcfs4EE_MRxBJ9xCbRaaWI3yZn_uQjzb5s5CQD1FdKK8x9YlDtfJWcPB0yOl1Vpv4ARPDIfXV4ENKoG5Jtr2esX_vFJcpvVqeLSca2W1MmZzencIzdDs0t2NGZoKlPJk9LkQdc5mReSzSrd_DMc0m8G8NnWj_jyoXV6qZhX0SGCMeXkKjgeYdwyOBfyIwOi_vokRWPq3_n9USM1DJPJep6wPeJhTGL2AJbwoTIVO-QdnalEhhnSooz74Y7jnrHOiSNDMVMxP93V4wSgMQbw ) , or people are spending more and more time talking to ChatGPT, or things along those lines. Is there just a very concrete, at the end of the day, all this rests on more and more engagement, and if we’re losing that, none of this matters?*

*ES:* Yeah, ultimately. But look, Instagram, 3 billion MAU, Threads is growing-

*Still growing, all growing.*

*ES:* They’ve got Meta AI, they’ve got surface areas here, I don’t know that engagement is an issue for them. I think, again, unless you’re thinking out 5 years, 10 years, but my sense is, I don’t think this is an existential threat, I think it’s just they may get unseated. But the thing is, OpenAI doesn’t seem to be operating with that much urgency in terms of building a principal, durable, growth oriented, viable business.

The New Privacy Environment

*How much is Meta relying on fingerprinting? I’ve given them a lot of credit ( https://stratechery.com/2024/metas-ai-abundance/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..XnZL4e87M8cJ8xS3E_2Qx_d4nkqVD6YyskUlyvxUrnczH0eVHXFT8iXOPiA1Ss3g_MUqsbmvFVagtMve--8XxENnwD4BLnk1bf86O7kuscajrVbFUjOFyjpMyW-znz8-fE12PnSRhxdzKvfWO0jZ3CEohu_LGxOOfjzJ4KZFZazWzlFWPe1xwmRhBBdlPVH2PcGL3mPqA-URBqVnfN2iZNQfxT4wOp8K5ddnwuVkWeE0XnBCYgt56B0EzA1fR_NHgjcqiCK-CWEQq0aG-rfnEdG_r1Y3TvHaiflqM1GI18cycQ9BNnWI4zyW85iW0nQLHX7WcRx4XDeB3E8Nw5fx_Q ) of this probabilistic targeting, and sort of getting out from under Apple permanently, there’s some pushback from folks that, “No, it’s just because Apple’s not policing the fingerprinting, they’re still doing deterministic just through all these signals, your IP address”, things along those lines, what’s your view on that?*

*ES:* Define fingerprinting? You can’t, that’s the problem. And the thing is, I’ve been writing this series of articles, the Privacy Shuffle ( https://mobiledevmemo.com/the-privacy-shuffle-part-4-google-shutters-privacy-sandbox/ ).

*Yep. Do you want to get to Google’s shifts in those regard also?*

*ES:* But this isn’t a front-and-center concern anymore, we’ve got an administration here that is not prioritizing that and so, I think what I’d be doing if I was Meta — and not saying violate people’s privacy, certainly I would not recommend to do that — but I would probably take advantage of this environment. And what I mean by that, again, is not just blatantly violate people’s privacy, but you need to make the case for why — you should proactively define fingerprinting, because the reason Apple didn’t want to do it is they wanted to give themselves total agency to police in any way they wanted to.

The thing is, what Apple’s facing now is pushback on ATT, they were accused of anti-competitive behavior by the French Competition Authority ( https://www.reuters.com/technology/french-antitrust-regulator-fines-apple-150-million-euros-over-privacy-tool-2025-03-31/ ) , German authorities made a preliminary finding of the same ( https://www.reuters.com/technology/apple-loses-german-antitrust-appeal-opening-door-greater-controls-2025-03-18/ ) , they’ve got cases going in the European Union. Now, I don’t like celebrating European Union incursion into American commerce—

*(laughing) It’s so true.*

*ES:* But I have to hold my nose in this case-

*At least this is actually an antitrust violation, that’s a big improvement.*

*ES:* Right, exactly. So I think what that did was it set a ceiling on what they can do, it set a ceiling. They can’t go further than ATT, and in fact, they may have to reverse some of the applications of ATT. So if that set the ceiling, well then you know where that is.

Now, the only thing that could change that is if after this administration you get a swing of the pendulum in the other direction, and you do have Lina Khan playing an outsized role, or Tim Wu playing an outsized role, or any of these people playing an outsized role, who are very vociferously anti-big tech and then they get to define what fingerprinting is. So, I think you say, fingerprinting is when you have some level of certainty that who we think this person is, is that person. It’s not that we think of this group of people, that person exists in that group, that’s a very different thing and the thing — what Apple tried to do is said, “Okay, you’ve got a group of people and you have some level of confidence that this individual’s in that group, that’s fingerprinting”, and actually, what fingerprinting should be is a level of confidence that this set of characteristics means it’s this individual person.

*How does this tie into Google finally abandoning privacy sandbox ( https://www.adweek.com/media/googles-privacy-sandbox-is-officially-dead/ ) ? And is that just another example of the environment changing, and it was always kind of a bad idea that actually favored Google anyway, and this is all a good thing?*

*ES:* It favored Google, deprecating cookies absolutely was going to favor Google, that was just going to drive more revenue through its O&O, owned and operated, which is higher margin. Look, I’ve talked about the Google’s network business is vestigial, it is.

*All it is just a headache inducer for them at this point ( https://stratechery.com/2025/google-remedy-decision-reasonable-remedies-the-google-patronage-network/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..v2TznI5JfpEKuF4yDyPWcGDbBC1DFg6x-wMXpoLijiHkGlOlSsxV-LEiHDoclj2npgYUIp_NmttnjYKchmQYcW7VLFjPQ2s6Y6mJLDDLooYffexs3qwVegnAK_YBHSJ5PpSd_s7hhDE0-7vOh275tDYT5gqNlWGIZLchwbLHUehYyBgItmp_Ff0GOKTZzF46r8HKgHPc4MyXplLwjKYhWl1Xs92uHqy-cOsPIJg4xtFZlQ8fJRLLN-CNlabfyIP5EF5TKvEu5c5yyr5L5hJUua8Vtvz6zIEgcL20gLbgy9HbGeG7ypxTxLfASzb1GxtjI2vq3s-AOZNJw4J4DXNEaA ).*

*ES:* Right, it’s a massive albatross around their neck and the thing is, they said this in earnings, they said every major part of our business grew double digits, Network was down.

*It’s been down for years.*

*ES:* They just said it, it’s been down for every quarter.

*Quarter after quarter.*

*ES:* I think it’s the 13th consecutive quarter it was down. And so, again, it’s just like this albatross around their neck. I think they’re probably happy to, at the very least, just deprioritize it, if not jettison it completely. I don’t know that they’ll do that at this point, they’re not going to be forced to.

*Everyone on the web should be begging that they don’t, but sometimes you get what you wish for and you’d regret it.*

*ES:* Well, I think actually what they’ve done is settle into the best case scenario for everyone because what’s happening is they’re not being forced to divest it, which probably means there’s some obligation to leave it intact. I mean, if they just completely shut it down, that might raise competition concerns. But now all of these open web players just get to sue them. Right? Because what happened was a judge decided that, “Look, if you bring a lawsuit against Google, you get to benefit from the fact that it was found to have violated antitrust law.” And so that’s just accepted as fact in your lawsuit in the proceedings. So we start from that point, and so now all these companies just get to get some money from them. That’s probably better for everybody.

*Right, everyone gets a payoff, that’s Google’s a real modus operandi ( https://stratechery.com/2024/friendly-google-and-enemy-remedies/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..MsbZdhoS9vdPdbdlA695AsZ4EbFmwvtY_EhLQ8XHRO5fBrBczYAHen-g4SwVrwOX95aIfNIsBnSuUwJyWmhCx72Io99M6g2YqAoQZQxcCeloTzLFYJ1ySgoRKkfgAXXDfKOt6xZGMkyTdCfsGB3y7QIkFsTLC9mENlm5Hz1ySZqrCl3ZGIm85a5fVk71C0ydY2GIZ5KmzEbgcRLqE0Akf5Dvyof3Z93Co8XyYBKea9ZXD8FkIgtM2RwwYPBYMgHW5XDMwHxZmO2P9iE9MWBLe8lxH1-w8A-82pPHR-MQb2-c5ziLNtMUiba_Qa4wmxl23ZYajv0ZOv7acQcZLSa8jw ) , “Look, we’ll pay everyone off, at the end of the day, it’s worth it to maintain our position”.*

*ES:* But that’s probably in everyone’s best interest, right? Because no one has served, we talked about this last time, no one has served it.

*This is sort the point of the decision with the Apple, the search placement is Google — it was a total validation of Google’s strategy, which is Google has bought everyone off to such an extent, and this is a pushback I always got on this point is, “Why would Google pay for search placement if they were going to win anyways?” — this is the answer. They guarantee that Apple would enter as a competitor, and more broadly, they basically have now been judiciously affirmed as a structural component of the online economy that cannot be harmed.*

*ES:* Right, while also reifying their own superiority with their search engine. Because again, Microsoft said, “If we gave away a hundred percent of the revenue that we got from being the default, we wouldn’t be able to offer Apple as much money as Google can”. There are companies that have switched away, there are browser companies that have switched away from Google, Firefox switched away from Google as a default at one point.

*Yeah, so did Yahoo.*

*ES:* They went back because the retention wasn’t high enough and so the thing is they proved that they are still — yes, that’s true, they had bought themselves into this position of being essential, but they also proved that well, they also have the best product and they’re essential from the other direction too, that these companies can’t afford to not have them being in there.

Why Agentic Commerce is a Mirage

*Why do you think that agentic commerce is a mirage ( https://mobiledevmemo.com/agentic-commerce-is-a-mirage/ ) ? I know already touched on some of these points, but I think it’s worth stating explicitly.*

*ES:* Well, so that article predates the affiliate one.

*You referenced it before, but we’re pretending like it’s fresh.*

*ES:* But let me die with you on this hill that when someone says agentic, semantically that means the commerce is done by the agent, it’s done autonomously by the agent. You give the agent your credit card and you say, “Okay, you’ve got an allowance of 500 bucks a month, buy me some cool stuff” — that is a mirage, that’s not going to happen, and there’s a lot of reasons for that. One is that you’re not going to — there’s too much risk in really high intent stuff, so you’re not going to have it buy a car for you. The stuff that actually requires a lot of research where the agent-

*The whole buying a plane ticket just cracks me up every time, that’s the last thing that’s going to AI buy for me.*

*ES:* No way, no chance. I have different rules for where I want to sit based on that airline, there’s no chance I’m giving that up to an agent and so the higher intent the product is that you want to buy, the less likely you are to give an agent that responsibility because the risk is higher.

Well, also, those are the products that benefit the most from the research. So I’ll do the research and then I’ll go to united.com, I don’t need you to buy the thing and the lower intent the thing is, then the less it benefits from research, so I don’t need an agent to do that for me either. I can go to Instacart and set up recurring purchases already, I don’t really care. You could, okay, buy me toilet paper, there’s not a lot of risk there, but there’s also not a lot of value being generated in having that buy it on my behalf. It’s an easier thing to just instrument to set it up.

So that was the original point I was making there is, semantically, the idea of agentic commerce is not realistic. But I think it’s fair to say, “Well, OpenAI is saying Instant Checkout is agentic commerce, and they get to define it” — okay, fine. But even if we allow that, I just don’t think they’re going to win over Amazon and Amazon’s 40% of US e-commerce.

*And why is it? You talked to the Amazon point before, “The incremental revenue isn’t worth having the whole buffalo”, a big part of that is advertising where basically more than their profit from e-commerce comes from advertising now. Is there a bit where this is all fine and well, or did Amazon sort of make themselves maybe a little more fragile and unable to respond because they’ve developed this sort of dependence on advertising?*

*ES:* I think that’s an interesting question. Maybe they get disrupted because they just can’t de-tether from advertising and it’s just such an attractive source of revenue.

*Which is so dependent on them owning the relationship and what’s happening on their site.*

*ES:* I think that’s fair. I think I’d make another point in favor them not embracing it, which is that they will embrace it, they’re just going to embrace it with their own proprietary tools. Rufus ( https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html?nodeId=Tvh55TTsQ5XQSFc7Pr ) , Amazon said it’s $10 billion of expected incremental sales in 2025, that’s a lot of money. And so I think if they could embrace it, they could just embrace it on platform and have it generate the same benefits for them. It’s just not some third-party agent that’s doing it, it’s their own agent.

*So you don’t think there will be a Amazon-ChatGPT deal, even with Walmart jumping in, it’s not going to force their hand?*

*ES:* It’s very different profiles of companies. I mean, Amazon is all e-commerce, Walmart’s percentage of e-commerce is a small minority. Walmart did $4.4 billion in advertising in 2024, the only reason the advertising revenue numbers on a yearly basis, $4.4 billion in advertising revenue in 2024 against $15.5 billion net income, Amazon did $56 billion in advertising on $59 billion of net income.

*But I mean, the Walmart e-commerce deal now is really good, they’ve finally figured out the local thing. You order something, if you want something right away, you’re actually more likely, even with Amazon really nailing next day or whatever it might be, Walmart is next hour and just go on there. Actually, this is a perfect example. There’s a possibility that Walmart, on ChatGPT, is going to introduce this actually pretty fantastic e-commerce platform with real meaningful advantages in some areas to a whole cohort of people that never would’ve thought to try Walmart otherwise.*

*ES:* It’s true, but I think that also highlights just how much more important incremental transactions are to Walmart relative to Amazon. Amazon doesn’t need them, Walmart does.

*But they don’t want to lose strength and they don’t want to lose customers though.*

*ES:* Yeah, fair. But I mean, I think they’ve got a stranglehold, Amazon is a diversified business, you’ve got the subscription with Prime and you’ve got all these other touch points for on video. So I think my sense is the value of that direct customer relationships supersedes anything else and actually, if you would cannibalize that by embracing a partnership with OpenAI, you’d be destroying a lot more value than you’d be generating and maybe that’s a challenging place to be because maybe they just can’t stop. They can’t stop the consumer embrace of chatbots and they’re probably not going to be able to offer that much of a compelling chatbot experience in the Amazon because it doesn’t make sense.

*Once you’re on Amazon, you know what you’re doing.*

*ES:* Right, you know what you’re doing. Or you could use Rufus for discovery stuff, but you’re not going there to do product research maybe. So there could be an argument here that they’re in a very dominant position, but that position would require some sort of wholesale reinvention of the customer relationship to keep the chatbot threat at bay. My sense is if I was just going to bet, that they probably can overcome that, but I think you could probably easily make the argument against that. Like, no, they can’t because this is going to be a transformative social shift.

*Right, I agree with that, I think that makes sense.*

Netflix Advertising

*You did write last summer, and I was really annoyed I didn’t write this at the time, I didn’t really pick up on until this quarter, “ Netflix’s advertising pivot is complete ( https://mobiledevmemo.com/netflixs-advertising-pivot-is-complete/ ).” What was that pivot? Tell me more.*

*ES:* Well, they pivoted to their own proprietary ad tech stack, they’ve got their own ad tech. It’s live now across all their ad geos.

*And why was that so important?*

*ES:* Because they were never going to be able to build a modern, functional, high performance ads platform if they were relying on a third party ad server. They were basically doing this as a means of spinning up a brand advertising business very quickly, which they did. So to their credit, they did that. But I think the reality is, if you want to compete for the actual prize of the actual crown of the digital advertising marketplace is SMB direct response ad budgets. That’s the scalable piece, that’s the substantial piece, and if you want to compete for that, you have to have your own ad platform, you have to be integrating with your own measurement, you have to be building proprietary ad formats, and you have to be providing targeting that’s actually actionable, and so I think they were always going to have to do this, and they’ve done it, they’ve instituted it and so I think that was the pivot. It was more like an infrastructure pivot, but they’ve done that, but I think it was also like a wholesale mentality pivot, which they did manage to complete.

*This is where I mentioned we would tie this in before, so let me sort of complete the bow here. What Netflix did is they suddenly completely — arguably, they were late to decide to do advertising, but once they decided, now today it’s been a couple years and they’re where they should be — but they didn’t just sit on their hands and say, “Yeah, we’re going to figure it out in the long run”, they got something out there and that’s something out there, which you pointed out all along, was not going to monetize as well as they said it was, it was probably going to diminish ARPU, not be neutral, and I think there was still benefit all along in that it let them increase prices I think on the paid tiers more than they might’ve otherwise. It obviously drew new users.*

*But the big thing is they launched, they launched within months of saying they were going to do it, and now they’re in a great spot right now and I think that they have other issues in terms of engagement and things along those lines just like anyone else but this is going to be a huge tailwind, I think for a long time going forward. And it’s downstream of just launching it and launching the wrong product and then forcing yourself to iterate and get it better. Real object lesson there for a company we were discussing earlier.*

*ES:* Yeah, but I think they’ve scaled it too. So November 6th, they published this blog post where they reported this new metric that they invented called Monthly Active Viewers ( https://x.com/eric_seufert/status/1986541667617296690?s=20 ) , where they try to estimate given an account like an average household size, how many people are actually watching the ads. They came up with 190 million, my own personal model for the ad-supported MAU-growth gets into 116 million, that’s a lot. And they talked about the growth in this earnings, they’re seeing strong growth in ads revenue.

*That was the big thing this summer, I think that was the first earnings call, or it might’ve been in the spring where before they were talking about ads, “Yeah, it’s there, it’s immaterial”, now, it was a material contributor to their forecasts, and that’s a big important shift.*

*ES:* They more than doubled their ads revenue. That’s from a small base, and those numbers got leaked in the Wall Street Journal from their strategy meeting, they said it’d be like $9 billion in revenue by 2030. Okay, that’s not going to change the world, but that’s a pretty meaningful amount of money that’s basically all profit. So I think the pivot is complete, and I think they’re executing against the strategy, and I think the strategy is the right one now.

*Eric, I have five more questions, I’m worried if we asked another one, we’re going to go a million miles longer. Let’s do this, let’s talk sooner rather than later next time and hopefully by that time, ChatGPT will have an ad product and then we’ll really have something to talk about.*

*ES:* We can talk about it, yeah, we could finally talk about ChatGPT ads.

subject: Linear: move fast with little process (with first engineering manager Sabin Roman)
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/linear-move-fast-with-little-process

Listen now on Apple [ https://substack.com/redirect/f0ffff81-44f5-4e88-8601-c42209cb3a81?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ], Spotify [ https://substack.com/redirect/e1d1ee38-5093-427d-bcb2-e65c24c390ff?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ], and YouTube [ https://substack.com/redirect/1229f31b-8cc4-4877-8c24-d9c8f4d4ffee?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ].
Brought to you by:
• Launch Darkly [ https://substack.com/redirect/8f4d6ff5-910c-4f1c-8419-f299755edb85?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] — a platform for high-velocity engineering teams to release, monitor, and optimize great software. 
• Sevalla [ https://substack.com/redirect/06b9f786-468b-440e-972b-f2c027f3f8ab?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] — Deploy anything from preview environments to Docker images.
• WorkOS [ https://substack.com/redirect/8f52ba77-5497-43ce-b960-c463c5243ee7?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ] — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS.
—
Linear is a small startup with a big impact: 10,000+ companies use their project and issue-tracking system, including 66% of Forbes Top 50 AI companies. Founded in 2019, the company raised $52M in funding and is  profitable, and full-remote. How did they pull this off with just 25 engineers?
On today’s episode of The Pragmatic Engineer, I’m joined by fellow Uber alum, Sabin Roman, now the first Engineering Manager at Linear. Linear, known for its powerful project and issue-tracking system, streamlines workflows throughout the product development process.
In our conversation today, Sabin and I compare building projects at Linear versus our experiences at Uber. He shares insights into Linear’s unique approaches, including:
How Linear handles internal communications
The “goalie” program to address customer concerns and Linear’s zero bug policy
How Linear keeps teams connected despite working entirely remotely
An in-depth, step-by-step walkthrough of a project at Linear
Linear’s focus on quality and creativity over fash shipping 
Titles at Linear, Sabin’s learnings from Uber, and much more!
Takeaways
My biggest takeaways from this conversation:
1. Linear has a preference for creativity over process – when the goal is creativity! Linear is intentionally light on engineering processes when they build new products: their goal is to communicate using “principles” rather than “guidebooks.” The company manages to do this thanks to a mix of hiring engineers with strong product skills and still being a small enough company (60 people and 25 engineers at the time of writing). 
2. Full remote is hard – especially for managers! And yet, there are payoffs to this approach. Sabin has been managing a full-remote team for two years, and this is how he puts it:
“It’s harder for managers to manage remote teams. It just is. It’s harder to build that trust and that connection. To get a pulse on what troubles them and what motivates them. But ultimately, my job as a manager is not to make my life easier.
I really wish that more managers would be more open to working remotely, because it does make your team’s life better. Your team can focus better. It allows flexibility.”
3. Starting a zero-bug policy is hard, but it’s much easier to continue once in place. Sabin admits that he was initially skeptical about whether it was feasible to implement an actual zero-bug policy.
However, after the initial few months of fixing existing bugs, he now sees that it works, and it works without overloading engineers. Part of the success seems to be in giving engineers the freedom to both decide when a bug should be considered a bug – and giving them autonomy in deciding how they use their time.
4. Despite working at Uber for 7 years, Sabin brought relatively few approaches from this large company to Linear.  Reflecting on why Uber and Linear are so different: a lot of it comes down to size, and the effectiveness of communication with a smaller team. As Sabin put it:
“You can’t underestimate the difference it makes for still being a startup. That takes away a lot of the challenges.”
This is a good reminder for anyone moving from Big Tech to a smaller company: don’t expect the same processes to work! We covered more on this topic in the deepdiveHiring software engineers from Big Tech [ https://substack.com/redirect/26314c54-a726-4373-a7e6-0284c01aee1b?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ].
Timestamps
(00:00 [ https://substack.com/redirect/40d014d0-2eaf-4a95-854f-af3560afa6bc?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ]) Intro
(01:41 [ https://substack.com/redirect/3b3a5264-d2ad-4ad8-bc6a-55a1697947cd?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ]) Sabin’s background
(02:45 [ https://substack.com/redirect/8cdbb8dd-5896-41a1-b0b4-f86bdc3cca84?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ]) Why Linear rarely uses e-mail internally
(07:32 [ https://substack.com/redirect/5dfec9fe-b28e-41c4-ade2-306799f2a0f5?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ]) An overview of Linear's company profile
(08:03 [ https://substack.com/redirect/2a80b63f-ef5b-4696-8809-c77aa8daad6a?j=eyJ1IjoiMXpiYW9pIn0.x_isyNDJZBwm1T2P5h--XwU8TVqNOVesUrZse-mL6YE ]) Linear’s tech stack
